item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Thank you for letting us know, the image has been restored.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Hi, author here.

That image was taken by me. Not sure if my word counts as proper sourcing, but…

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not all articles need to tell a story - they just need to provide something to catch the reader's interest and draw them along. This does neither, so -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do those who have been affected by an instance have to be amnesticized before being detained? Unless and until the Foundation finds a cure for the disappearing-money issue, these folks are essentially set to be detainees/test subjects for life anyway. They shouldn't be getting the amnestic treatment until after a cure is found so they can then be released back into the wild.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like the whole thing about monitoring everyone who handles money is both excessive for the effect and quite impossible a task. I'd believe it more if the article stated that due to the ubiquity of cash handling and no current knowledge on how instances are created, it's considered uncontained and potentially uncontainable. I feel that would also make it a bit more intimidating as well.

There's a few too many reference notes in the middle, on top of already lengthy containment regulations, I'd prefer those in parentheses, if they have to be there.

It seems like the Foundation doesn't have enough of these to test them enough to know this much about the SCP (edit-they've apparently come across quite a few; since they'd know how many they've found or have contained, a number would provide some sense of scale). Sometimes it's like reading the author's notes on it, rather than what could be conclusions arrived at through research. For example, how do they know the D-class has acquired the effect? It seems like the nature of it is hard to determine in the first place, yet the test log states it as if it's conclusively known.

It's also a bit weird how the effect involves losing all currency except the SCP, yet the articles states the SCP itself can be lost as a means of transferring ownership. I'm either confused by it or there's a few contradictions to how it works, which could be remedied by not having the article state these things with such certainty, which is implausible for the item in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A Scp which wouldn't affect canada. 1+ for letting me sleep at night.

item.title: No vote
item.content:encoded: The special containment procedures were a slog to get through, with the text being too reliant on the description of the SCP and I felt it was too lengthy. However, the actual description of the SCP was good, and kept me interested until the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's to be inferred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Future Euros will have same effects on Euro currency, not dollars, right?
Just to clarify

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same here. I'm left wondering if the Foundation's concealment efforts are responsible for the fact that we still make pennies (despite the cost-ineffective nature of the process and the near-uselessness of the coins), which I think is an amazing idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I can upvote it, as there just isn't enough to the concept. I feel like if it had another part to its effects, it would give the skip more depth. Right now I feel like it's a little too bare.

It's good but not great. I like it enough that I won't downvote though. I'll vote neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<famous Eta Carinae headscratch> (usually not a good sign) ;>
Okay. I rather like it, but… yes, a bit too long before the anomalous effect 'kicks in'; and mostly, I'm left wondering how the hell the Foundation first found out about this anomaly????
Everyone handles money. Everyone loses cash. I know, it's been going on since 1967, so they've had time to figure it out, but… how did they spot the problem to start with?
You don't have to include this into your skip - I'm just sort of thinking aloud here. I always pay attention to the believability of stories, and this seems to me a major weakness. Since it doesn't seem to bother anyone else, you are free to ignore me!

Oh, what the hell. Headscratch not a bad sign this time: upvote. Still well done, I'd say!

item.title: Re: Future Pennies
item.content:encoded:
I find the SCP to be very intriguing. The procedures could be a tad tedious at times, but it was very well written. I think it could use a little more trimming, but other then that, VERY good!

+1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this comes off as too much build up and not enough pay off for my tastes. Not only do you have a set of massive containment procedures, which could still probably be pruned down as I find that they bog down the article right out the gate, but when the reader finally arrives at the anomaly itself, they are treated to money what makes you lose money.

Granted, there is an irony here that I think has potential to make a wonderful article if given the proper treatment, but for me, this article was not it.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that sounds great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll try to fix up a few of these later. I'm thinking of maybe removing the part about affecting dead people, and saying (maybe in the same paragraph) that those who already own an instance can't steal the eventual effect from someone else if the coin is transferred.

Would that somehow work? Also, I'm really glad you like it.

Edit:

Ownership cannot be transferred to animals, corpses, those already owning an instance of SCP-2534-1, or those who have already received SCP-2534-1's effect.

Does this work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this idea, it's very interesting. A few points though:

A week prior to an SCP-2534-1 instance's estimated two-year mark, it is to be held by a D-Class personnel

Why only a week before? Wouldn't it make more sense to transfer the effect to the D-Class as soon as you get the coin?
Also what if you hand all the coins to one person? How does that work?
After the two-year mark, the effect manifests, and it just becomes a regular penny right?
How do the effects work after the subject dies? Corpses can't handle cash.
The whole "bank robbery" thing feels a bit weird since the CEO doesn't technically own the land the bank is on, it's company property (at least from what I remember). Also it really doesn't fit with the rest of the stuff about "money in the subject's possession".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually pretty neat. If it resonates with me, it's most likely because I've always been in the habit of picking coins up off the ground. (Found a dollar bill yesterday!)

But I also like the meticulous containment procedures, and the fact that it seems to be focused around US currency, yet is spreading. Upvote to stave off deletion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any ideas on a fix for that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. How did I not catch that?

I'll fix that once I get off of mobile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote, but I appreciate what you were going for.

Any who, one thing struck me: you say the money disappearances always appear non-anomalous, but then you have tests where money just disappears from peoples pockets. That don't sound non-anomalous to me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found it kinda charming. It feels anti-penny and I like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't really grab me. I feel it's a little dry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All the edits have made this a no-vote on my part, though I'm not too into it regardless. For me, even with its competence, it seems like a setup to the robbery part. The testing was mildly interesting but I couldn't get into the article by that point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem! That kind of editing is actually one of my favorite things to do, so thanks for giving me a chance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I saw this as kind of harsh, but now I understand where you're coming from.

Hopefully with Sibyl's rewrite and my revision of the Incident Log, this piece is better now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, you edited a lot more than I expected. I really like where you took it!

I can't thank you enough for this. It looks really great now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I gave it a shot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, yep. Anything particularly wrong with that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, an image named IMG_1843[1]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure. I'd be down if you could condense some stuff. Thanks a lot for the offer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The effect is all right, but I'd say the description needs to be cut down by at least a third. Also, I'd lose the 2A/2B business and just say "subjects."

I'm pretty good at condensing stuff like this, if you don't mind someone else taking a crack at it. You could always revert it afterward if you hated it. Is that something you'd be interested in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is always two (2) years

Other than the "two (2)" and that the paragraphing was a bit all over the place, I enjoyed it. Although it can be polished just a bit more (I don't really have anything specific, it just didn't seem as good as it could have been, maybe it's just the paragraphing, although probably a few other things.) Nonetheless, +1 from me.

Edit: Ehh, after re-reading it, I'll give it a neutral vote until it is fixed. And i defintely do see potential in this.
Edit Edit: I think Cumaeans rewrite got my upvote back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is way, way too excessive in terms of presentation of minutiae. Between that, the proliferation of subdesignations that's an increasing problem around here ("SCP-2534-2A" is the point at which I find myself having to refer back to what terms mean what), and the fact that the effect of the item isn't revealed until the last quarter of the article, this was barely readable to me. Once I finally reached the buried lede, I wasn't terribly interested in magic currency robbery as the be-all end-all of the story.

And then there's this:

Folks, look at this as a wake up call. Though this event was nothing to sneeze at, this proves that SCP-2534-1 could have some massive consequences if it were to fall into the wrong hands. Imagine if this happened at a federal reserve. If that were to happen, SCP-2534-1 could cause a serious dent in the US economy. I'm requesting a revision to the containment procedures. -Captain A███, head of MTF-Rho-7

This is one of my least favorite devices on the wiki. If the author, via a barely-fictionalized mouthpiece, has to explain directly to the reader why they should care about the story, the writer has not executed properly. The piece itself should be doing that job.

item.title: Future Pennies
item.content:encoded: It's here.
